commit.message:
feat(concourse): graceful spot worker drain via lifecycle hooks and IMDS watcher (#4453)

* feat(concourse): graceful spot worker drain via lifecycle hooks and IMDS watcher

Spot-terminated Concourse workers were leaving stale registrations on
web nodes because there was no mechanism to drive the SIGUSR2 retirement
flow before the instance disappeared.

Changes:
- Add concourse-worker-drain script (sends SIGUSR2, waits for exit)
- Add concourse-worker-spot-watch systemd service: polls EC2 IMDS every
  5s for spot/termination-time; calls drain on detection
- Add concourse-worker-lifecycle-hook systemd service: polls ASG API
  every 10s for Terminating:Wait state; drains (540s timeout) then calls
  CompleteLifecycleAction CONTINUE
- Install awscli on worker instances (required by lifecycle-hook script)
- Set connection_drain_timeout=5m to bound drain window (was 1h default)
- Add ASG LifecycleHook resources in Pulumi (heartbeat_timeout=600s,
  default_result=CONTINUE) for each worker ASG
- Add autoscaling IAM permissions (DescribeAutoScalingInstances,
  DescribeLifecycleHooks, CompleteLifecycleAction) to base worker policy
- Add ADR 0008 documenting the decision not to use ephemeral=True and
  the risks found in concourse/concourse issues #2719 and #2827

ephemeral=True was considered but rejected: under high worker load the
TSA heartbeat can be delayed, causing the web node to mark the worker
stalled and immediately exit the worker process, killing running builds.
The Concourse team removed ephemeral from their own docker-compose for
this same reason (see issue #2719).

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(concourse): remove BindsTo from lifecycle-hook service

The lifecycle-hook service had BindsTo=concourse.service. During a
scale-in event the handler drains the worker (SIGUSR2), causing
concourse.service to become inactive. BindsTo propagates that
deactivation immediately, terminating the lifecycle-hook script before
it can call complete-lifecycle-action. AWS then waits the full 600-second
heartbeat timeout before proceeding with instance termination.

Fix: remove BindsTo, retain After= for start ordering only. The script
handles the concourse-not-running case gracefully (drain is a no-op)
and always calls complete-lifecycle-action after drain returns, so it
does not need to be coupled to concourse.service lifecycle.

The spot-watch service correctly retains BindsTo: it only sleeps after
drain completes and has no post-drain API call to protect.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(concourse): remove BindsTo from spot-watch service; scope IAM CompleteLifecycleAction

spot-watch.service: remove BindsTo=concourse.service for consistency
with the lifecycle-hook fix. While the functional risk is lower (no
post-drain API call), BindsTo creates a race: tail --pid polls at ~1s
intervals, so there is a window where concourse has exited but drain
has not returned yet -- systemd could propagate the inactive state and
stop the spot-watch cgroup (including the drain subprocess) before it
finishes. Additionally, if concourse crashes and systemd restarts it,
BindsTo would stop spot-watch permanently (Restart=on-failure does not
trigger when stopped by systemd). Keeping only After= for start
ordering is correct; the drain script handles concourse-not-running
gracefully.

iam_policies/base.py: split autoscaling statement to scope
CompleteLifecycleAction with a StringLike condition on
autoscaling:AutoScalingGroupName matching 'concourse-worker-*'.
Describe* actions still require Resource:* (AWS does not support
resource-level permissions for those actions). This prevents a
compromised worker from completing lifecycle actions on unrelated ASGs.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Add MXFP8 XPU feasibility analysis

Document the investigation into MXFP8 support for Intel XPU via sycl-tla:
- Survey of all Xe dispatch policies in sycl-tla (gap: no grouped+FP8+scaling)
- float_ue8m0_t support (software fallback only on Intel Xe; no CUDA PTX)
- Intel Xe hardware limits (no native blockscaled MXFP8 XMX instruction)
- Three implementation options with effort/quality tradeoffs
- Recommendation: Option A (software dequant pre-pass) now, Option B
  (new sycl-tla MainloopXeL1StagedGroupFP8Scaling) as upstream contribution

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
perf: optimize closure call path in call_undefined_receiver0_inner

Three optimizations to reduce per-call overhead for repeated closure
invocations (e.g. the closure_counter benchmark):

1. Skip CreateMappedArguments trampoline when arguments is unused:
   Add a SKIP_MAPPED_ARGS TLS flag (cached via RtPtrs for fast access).
   When the callee has 0 formal parameters, set the flag before execute
   so the CreateMappedArguments handler returns JIT_UNDEFINED immediately
   instead of building a full arguments object with PropertyMap, callee,
   and iterator properties.

2. Cache Maglev entry pointer in CachedCalleeEntry:
   Add maglev_fn/maglev_reg_slots fields to the callee cache. On cache
   hit, call the Maglev code pointer directly via transmuted function
   pointer, bypassing maglev_executable_cache() RefCell lookup, lazy
   Arc<Mutex> transfer check, and CachedMaglevCode::execute overhead.
   The cache is populated on first successful Maglev dispatch and
   invalidated on deopt.

3. Skip heap truncation for non-heap results from 0-param closures:
   When skip_args is true and the result is a Smi (jit_result < JIT_HEAP_TAG),
   skip the heap length check entirely since no heap allocations occurred
   (CreateMappedArguments was skipped, and the closure only did Smi arithmetic).

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Fix false [DEGRADED] prefix on debug emails when time-aware fallback is used (#296)

* Initial plan

* fix: distinguish time-aware fallback from template fallback to prevent false [DEGRADED] email prefix

When both AI editorial providers fail, the system falls back to either:
1. Time-aware fallback (location-specific, generated from window data) — not degraded
2. Generic template fallback (hardcoded text) — degraded

Previously, `templateFallbackUsed` was incorrectly set to equal `fallbackUsed`,
causing time-aware fallbacks to be flagged as degraded. This meant the debug email
subject was incorrectly prefixed with '[DEGRADED]' even when the editorial content
was still location-specific.

Changes:
- Add `buildFallbackResult()` in fallbacks.ts that returns both text and
  `isTemplateFallback` flag
- Pass `templateFallbackUsed` explicitly through resolve-editorial.ts to
  buildDebugAiTrace instead of using fallbackUsed
- Add 3 new tests covering the key scenarios

commit.message:
fix: parallel fan-out/fan-in + labeled predecessor outputs

Problems fixed:
1. resolveNextNode only returned first edge target — fan-out to parallel
   branches (e.g. 毛泽东+马斯克) was silently dropped
2. Fan-in (join) missing — 巴菲特 ran without waiting for both predecessors
3. availableInputs flat-merged all outputs (key collisions, lost source info)
4. ctx.messages truncated to last 3 — downstream nodes got incomplete history

Fixes:
- Replace resolveNextNode with resolveNextNodes returning all valid targets
- Add getNodePredecessors + isNodeReady helpers for join detection
- Add pendingParallelNodes: string[] to ExecutionContext
- completeNode: fan-out → queue extra targets; fan-in → check all preds done
  before advancing; pop pendingParallelNodes when join not yet ready
- availableInputs now { globalContext, predecessorOutputs: { nodeLabel: output } }
  so each node clearly sees which output came from which predecessor
- Remove messages.slice(-3) — pass full message history

commit.message:
Add init container to backup management pod to fix PV permissions (#4)

* Add init container to backup management pod to fix PV permissions

Storage providers like Cinder and Longhorn create PVs with 755 root:root
permissions. The backup management pod runs postgres as uid 26, which
cannot write to the backup volume. Add an init container that runs as
root to chown/chmod the backup mount before the main container starts.

Modeled after the existing postgres StatefulSet init container pattern
(postgres_data_volume_init) and upstream awx-operator PRs #1805/#1998.

Claude assisted-by

* Apply suggestions from code review

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* Replace init container with fsGroup for backup PV permissions

Use pod-level securityContext.fsGroup: 26 instead of a privileged init
container to fix backup PVC ownership on non-OpenShift clusters. The
kubelet applies the group ownership change at the node level before any
container starts, which works correctly in PodSecurity restricted
namespaces and avoids running any container as UID 0.

Move junction-spanning filter to translation step; peptides as TSV (#22)

* Move junction-spanning filter to translate step; peptides are now TSV not FASTA

- translate_peptides.py: extract junction-spanning 9-mers directly from contigs;
  output TSV (contig_key, start_nt, peptide) instead of 16-mer FASTA
- run_mhcflurry.py: reads peptides TSV; removed all frame/9-mer extraction logic
- translate.smk / predict.smk: wired to new TSV intermediate
- generate_report.py: uses contig_key/start_nt/peptide columns directly; fixed
  pipeline diagram text and _load_contigs docstring
- config.yaml / test_config.yaml: removed stale translation section and
  mhcflurry.peptide_length
- tests: rewrote test_translate_peptides.py for extract_spanning_9mers;
  rewrote test_run_mhcflurry.py for TSV I/O and binder classification (21 pass)
- docs/METHODS.md: updated §5 with new contig/frame visualisation and spanning
  condition table
- docs/DISCUSSIONS.md: added section on upstream_nt=26 vs 24

Closes #20

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* Add regression test with golden snapshot to detect unintended output changes

- test_regression.py: compares pipeline outputs (junction counts, contig
  counts, peptide counts, strong-binder peptide set) against a committed
  golden snapshot; fails if anything changes unexpectedly
- golden/test_metrics.json: initial snapshot from the issue #20 pipeline
  (1371 9-mers, 11 strong / 41 weak binders on chr22 test dataset)

The golden was first created from the main branch to confirm the difference
introduced by issue #20 (+140 peptides, +6 weak binders, same 10 strong
binders), then updated to the new expected values.

Usage:
  pytest workflow/tests/test_regression.py        # CI check
  python workflow/tests/test_regression.py        # manual compare
  python workflow/tests/test_regression.py --update-golden  # after intentional change

commit.message:
feat: switch production database to PostgreSQL

requirements.txt:
  - Add psycopg2-binary>=2.9.9

backend/Dockerfile:
  - Builder stage: add libpq-dev (needed to compile psycopg2)
  - Runtime stage: add libpq5 (psycopg2 runtime dependency)

docker-compose.prod.yml:
  - Add postgres:16-alpine service with healthcheck
  - DATABASE_URL injected as postgresql://noos:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@db:5432/noos
  - backend depends_on db (service_healthy)
  - noos_pgdata volume for PostgreSQL data
  - noos_reports volume for generated reports

backend/.env.example:
  - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=CHANGE_ME (only credential needed in .env)
  - DATABASE_URL is now injected by compose (not set in .env)
  - Documents SQLite alternative for local dev

Local dev (docker-compose.yml) is unchanged — still uses SQLite.
Tests are unchanged — still use sqlite:///:memory: + StaticPool.
Alembic migrations work with both databases unchanged.

First-time setup on Orange Pi:
  cp backend/.env.example backend/.env
  # set POSTGRES_PASSWORD, SECRET_KEY, ADMIN_PASSWORD
  docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --build
